metadata.title: Software Managers' Operational Excellence Checklist
intended_use: Run monthly or quarterly engineering operations reviews
Track control status with named owners and concrete evidence

source.title: Software Managers' Guide to Operational Excellence
source.author: Kate Matsudaira

red.label: Missing or unreliable
criteria: Control is missing, unreliable, or only works because one person remembers it
Ownership, cadence, or evidence is unclear
The team is exposed to avoidable launch, incident, or recovery risk

yellow.label: Partial or inconsistent
criteria: Control exists but is incomplete, too manual, or not followed consistently
Evidence exists but is stale, fragmented, or hard to inspect in a review
The team can operate, but quality still depends too much on heroics

green.label: Effective and repeatable
criteria: Control is documented, implemented, and reviewed on a visible cadence
The team can launch, support, and recover services predictably

domains.name: Operational Foundations
domains.description: Managerial routines and engineering practices that reduce avoidable toil and stabilize delivery.

controls.name: Quality Assurance Investment
controls.description: Quality work has explicit capacity, ownership, and review instead of being squeezed in after feature delivery.
evidence_examples: Quarterly quality plan with named owners
Test ownership matrix by service or product area
Quality dashboard showing escaped defects and test health

scoring_criteria.red: Quality work is mostly reactive and gets cut when delivery pressure rises.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Quality work is planned in some teams, but ownership or coverage is inconsistent.
scoring_criteria.green: Quality work is deliberately planned, owned, and reviewed alongside feature delivery.

controls.name: Task Automation
controls.description: Recurring operational work such as deploy checks, report gathering, and routine remediation is automated first.
evidence_examples: Toil backlog ranked by hours saved
Automated operational jobs or scripts in production use

scoring_criteria.red: Teams still spend significant time on repetitive operational work that should be automated.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Important repetitive tasks are automated, but several high-friction workflows remain manual.
scoring_criteria.green: Automation is used systematically to remove recurring manual operational work.

controls.name: Engineering Process Standardization
controls.description: Core processes such as release, incident response, and post-incident follow-up are standard enough to scale across teams.
evidence_examples: Release checklist used by multiple teams
Standard incident workflow with clear handoffs

scoring_criteria.red: Engineering processes vary widely or are undocumented.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Core processes are documented but applied unevenly.
scoring_criteria.green: Engineering processes are standardized, improved regularly, and broadly followed.

controls.name: Performance Measurement
controls.description: Measure and track performance data to understand operational health.

scoring_criteria.red: Operational performance is not measured consistently.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Some performance data is tracked, but coverage or review discipline is incomplete.
scoring_criteria.green: Performance data is tracked, reviewed regularly, and used to drive improvements.

controls.name: Continuous Improvement Culture
controls.description: Foster a team culture that continuously improves operational quality and reliability.

scoring_criteria.red: Operational issues recur without structured learning or improvement follow-through.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Improvement efforts happen but are intermittent or leader-dependent.
scoring_criteria.green: Continuous improvement is expected, visible, and reinforced through regular follow-up.

domains.name: Launch Readiness
domains.description: A pre-launch operating checklist that verifies teams can see, support, and recover the service after release.

controls.name: Monitoring and Dashboards
controls.description: The team can see service health, business impact, and rollout behavior without scrambling for access during launch.
evidence_examples: Launch dashboard linked in release notes
Verified access list for on-call and launch participants
Recent alert test or dashboard smoke-check

scoring_criteria.red: Monitoring is missing, broken, or not accessible when needed.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Core dashboards exist but coverage or access is incomplete.
scoring_criteria.green: Monitoring and dashboards are reliable, accessible, and used during launches.

controls.name: Runbooks and Playbooks
controls.description: A responder outside the delivery team can follow the launch and recovery instructions without tribal knowledge.
evidence_examples: Dry-run or walkthrough completed by a second team

scoring_criteria.red: Operational knowledge is tribal or undocumented.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Runbooks exist but are outdated or hard for new responders to use.
scoring_criteria.green: Runbooks are current, understandable, and validated by people outside the authoring team.

controls.name: SLO Definition and Tracking
controls.description: The release has explicit service targets and the team can see whether the launch is burning error budget.
evidence_examples: SLO definition for the affected service
Error budget view used during launch
Recent SLO review with owner and action items

scoring_criteria.red: SLOs are not defined or are not measured.
scoring_criteria.yellow: SLOs exist for some services but are not used consistently.
scoring_criteria.green: SLOs are defined, tracked, and used to guide launch and reliability decisions.

controls.name: Disaster Recovery and Backups
controls.description: Disaster recovery plans and backups are in place and tested.

scoring_criteria.red: Backups or recovery plans are missing or untested.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Recovery assets exist but tests are infrequent or incomplete.
scoring_criteria.green: Recovery plans and backups are documented, tested, and trusted.

controls.name: Dependency Failure Behavior
controls.description: Dependency failure behavior is understood, including graceful degradation.

scoring_criteria.red: Teams do not understand how dependencies fail or recover.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Known failure scenarios exist but are not fully tested or documented.
scoring_criteria.green: Dependency failure modes are understood, documented, and handled with graceful degradation where needed.

controls.name: Load and Performance Testing
controls.description: Load and performance testing have been conducted.

scoring_criteria.red: Performance and load behavior are largely unknown.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Some testing exists but does not cover key launch scenarios.
scoring_criteria.green: Load and performance testing is completed and informs launch decisions.

controls.name: Compliance Readiness
controls.description: Compliance requirements such as security, privacy, and regulations are met.

scoring_criteria.red: Required compliance checks are missing or incomplete.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Compliance activities are present but not fully integrated into launch readiness.
scoring_criteria.green: Compliance requirements are reviewed, evidenced, and cleared before launch.

domains.name: Incident Management
domains.description: Practices that turn incidents into concrete reliability improvements instead of repeating the same failures.

controls.name: Incident Review Process
controls.description: Every significant incident is reviewed with a clear timeline, contributing factors, and owned follow-up actions.
evidence_examples: Review cadence for sev1 or sev2 incidents
Completed incident reviews with assigned actions

scoring_criteria.red: Incidents are resolved without formal review or learning.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Reviews occur, but not consistently or not deeply enough.
scoring_criteria.green: Incidents are reviewed through a repeatable retrospective or RCA process.

controls.name: Incident Follow-Up Tracking
controls.description: Reliability actions from incidents are tracked like delivery work and closed only with evidence.
evidence_examples: Incident action board with due dates
Named owners for each remediation item

scoring_criteria.red: Action items are not tracked or routinely abandoned.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Actions are tracked but closure is slow or incomplete.
scoring_criteria.green: Follow-up items are owned, tracked, and completed with visible accountability.

controls.name: Alert Volume Management
controls.description: Alert volume is measured and excessive alerting is addressed.

scoring_criteria.red: Alert noise is not measured or managed.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Alert volume is known, but cleanup is inconsistent.
scoring_criteria.green: Alert volume is measured and high-noise areas are actively reduced.

controls.name: Response Quality Review
controls.description: Incident response quality is reviewed, including SME coverage and focus on resolution.

scoring_criteria.red: Response quality is not reviewed and gaps are poorly understood.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Some response issues are recognized, but review is not systematic.
scoring_criteria.green: Response quality is reviewed regularly and used to improve coverage and resolution practices.

controls.name: Weekly Action Item Review
controls.description: Open incident action items are reviewed weekly.

scoring_criteria.red: Incident actions are not reviewed on a regular cadence.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Reviews happen but are irregular or incomplete.
scoring_criteria.green: Open incident actions are reviewed weekly until resolved.

domains.name: On-Call Management
domains.description: A sustainable on-call model that balances service safety with fairness and engineer wellbeing.

controls.name: On-Call Schedule Definition
controls.description: Coverage is explicit far enough in advance that managers can spot gaps, overload, or unfair rotation patterns.
evidence_examples: Published rota for the next 6 to 8 weeks
Written rotation length and handoff policy

scoring_criteria.red: On-call coverage is unclear or unmanaged.
scoring_criteria.yellow: A schedule exists but coverage assumptions or rotation rules are weak.
scoring_criteria.green: On-call scheduling is defined, visible, and predictable.

controls.name: Backup Response Plan
controls.description: Escalation to a secondary responder is explicit, tested, and fast enough to matter during a real incident.
evidence_examples: Escalation path documented in the paging tool
Secondary responder or manager backup flow
Recent escalation drill or real incident evidence

scoring_criteria.red: No reliable fallback exists for missed pages.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Backup coverage exists but is informal or untested.
scoring_criteria.green: Backup response paths are defined, understood, and tested.

controls.name: Responder Enablement
controls.description: On-call engineers have the required skills, tools, and permissions.

scoring_criteria.red: Responders lack access, skills, or tools to resolve incidents safely.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Most responders are prepared, but key gaps still exist.
scoring_criteria.green: Responders are equipped, trained, and authorized for on-call work.

controls.name: On-Call Pain Management
controls.description: On-call pain such as paging frequency and disruption hours is evaluated and minimized.

scoring_criteria.red: On-call burden is high and not actively managed.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Pain points are known but improvement work is sporadic.
scoring_criteria.green: On-call pain is measured and reduced through structured improvements.

controls.name: Feature Work Expectations
controls.description: Expectations are clear about feature work during on-call duty.

scoring_criteria.red: Feature expectations during on-call are unclear and create overload.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Expectations exist but vary by manager or team.
scoring_criteria.green: Teams have clear and fair expectations for feature work while on-call.

controls.name: Rotation Fairness
controls.description: Rotation selection is fair and well-structured.

scoring_criteria.red: On-call participation is uneven or perceived as unfair.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Rotation is mostly structured, but fairness concerns remain.
scoring_criteria.green: Rotation design is clear, balanced, and perceived as fair.

domains.name: Operational Data and System Health
domains.description: Operational visibility that helps managers connect service health, business events, and capacity risk.

controls.name: System and Business Dashboards
controls.description: A single operating view shows system health, customer impact, and the business signals most likely to explain change.
evidence_examples: Primary ops dashboard used in weekly review
Business KPI overlay for traffic or conversion impact
Service review pack shared with leadership

scoring_criteria.red: Health visibility is fragmented or missing.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Dashboards exist but do not provide a full operational picture.
scoring_criteria.green: Dashboards give reliable visibility into technical and business health.

controls.name: Dashboard Review Cadence
controls.description: Managers and team leads use dashboards in a fixed review cadence, not only during incidents.
evidence_examples: Action log linked to observed anomalies
Review notes showing trends and decisions

scoring_criteria.red: Dashboards exist but are rarely used in decision-making.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Dashboards are reviewed sometimes but without a steady cadence.
scoring_criteria.green: Dashboards are reviewed regularly and trigger operational action.

controls.name: Operational Awareness of Change Drivers
controls.description: Teams are aware of current feature flags, marketing campaigns, and sales events.

scoring_criteria.red: Operational teams lack visibility into business or product events affecting load and behavior.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Some change drivers are visible, but not in one reliable operating view.
scoring_criteria.green: Teams routinely know which product and business events may affect system health.

controls.name: Elastic Scaling and Threshold Awareness
controls.description: Systems scale elastically and operating thresholds are known.

scoring_criteria.red: Capacity limits are unclear and scaling is unreliable.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Scaling works in some scenarios, but thresholds are not well understood.
scoring_criteria.green: Elastic scaling behavior and critical thresholds are known, tested, and monitored.

domains.name: Customer Issue Tracking
domains.description: A manager-level view of recurring customer pain and whether engineering quality is improving or degrading.

controls.name: Customer Issue Volume Tracking
controls.description: Support, field, and app-store issues are grouped into a usable taxonomy so managers can see the real quality picture.
evidence_examples: Customer issue dashboard by category and severity
Shared taxonomy used by support and engineering

scoring_criteria.red: Customer issue data is not tracked consistently.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Issue tracking exists but lacks clean categorization or full coverage.
scoring_criteria.green: Customer issue frequency and type are tracked in a structured way.

controls.name: Customer Issue Trend Analysis
controls.description: Customer issue trends are reviewed over time so managers can see whether fixes are actually reducing pain.
evidence_examples: Weekly or monthly issue trend chart
Top recurring problems with trend direction
Quality review notes linking trend to actions

scoring_criteria.red: Issue trends are not analyzed over time.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Trend analysis exists but is inconsistent or too shallow.
scoring_criteria.green: Customer issue trends are reviewed and used to prioritize improvements.

controls.name: Quality Review in Feature Delivery
controls.description: Quality management is considered in the context of new features.

scoring_criteria.red: Feature planning and launch work do not consider operational quality risks.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Quality is considered for some features but not as a consistent practice.
scoring_criteria.green: Quality considerations are built into new feature planning and release decisions.

controls.name: Timeouts, Errors, and Crash Tracking
controls.description: Frequency of timeouts, errors, or crashes is tracked, especially in mobile environments.

scoring_criteria.red: Timeouts, crashes, and errors are not measured reliably.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Some error tracking exists, but coverage or segmentation is incomplete.
scoring_criteria.green: Timeouts, errors, and crashes are tracked and reviewed as core quality signals.

domains.name: Failover and Recovery
domains.description: The team can fail over, degrade gracefully, and restore service with known timings rather than hope.

controls.name: Recovery Plan Documentation and Testing
controls.description: Recovery steps are documented, tested, and recent enough that the manager would trust them in a real outage.
evidence_examples: Recovery plan with RTO and RPO targets
Recent restore or failover test evidence
Tabletop or game-day notes with follow-up actions

scoring_criteria.red: Recovery plans are undocumented or untested.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Recovery plans exist, but tests are incomplete or outdated.
scoring_criteria.green: Recovery plans are documented, tested, and reviewed on a regular cadence.

controls.name: Graceful Degradation
controls.description: Systems degrade gracefully when needed.

scoring_criteria.red: Failure modes tend to cause full or uncontrolled service loss.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Some graceful degradation exists, but important gaps remain.
scoring_criteria.green: Systems can degrade intentionally and predictably under stress or dependency failure.

controls.name: Full Recovery Time Measurement
controls.description: Managers know the current time to full recovery and whether recovery work is making that number better.
evidence_examples: MTTR or full recovery trend by service

scoring_criteria.red: Recovery time is not measured or improved deliberately.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Recovery time is measured after major events, but improvement is inconsistent.
scoring_criteria.green: Recovery time is measured routinely and reduced through targeted improvements.

domains.name: CI/CD, Testing, and Automation
domains.description: Operational delivery controls that keep releases safe, observable, and reversible.

controls.name: Deployment Quality Confidence
controls.description: Managers can look at a small set of release signals and decide whether the deployment is healthy enough to continue.
evidence_examples: Change failure rate by team or service
Release scorecard used in go or no-go decisions
Recent deployment review with follow-up actions

scoring_criteria.red: Deployments are risky and confidence is low.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Deployments are generally workable, but reliability varies by system or team.
scoring_criteria.green: Deployments are trusted because quality signals are strong and consistent.

controls.name: Gradual Rollouts
controls.description: Canary releases or feature flags are used for gradual rollouts.

scoring_criteria.red: Releases are mostly all-at-once with limited control.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Gradual rollout tooling exists but is not used consistently.
scoring_criteria.green: Canaries or feature flags are standard practice for risk-managed releases.

controls.name: Synthetic and Real User Monitoring
controls.description: The team can tell quickly whether the release broke key user journeys in production, not just internal health checks.
evidence_examples: Synthetic checks for top customer journeys
Real-user monitoring dashboard for critical flows
Coverage list of journeys monitored in production

scoring_criteria.red: Key user flows are not monitored end to end.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Some key flows are monitored, but coverage is incomplete.
scoring_criteria.green: Synthetic and real-user monitoring cover the most important user journeys.

controls.name: Observability and Instrumentation
controls.description: Systems have adequate observability and instrumentation.

scoring_criteria.red: Systems are hard to observe or debug in production.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Observability is present but uneven across systems.
scoring_criteria.green: Systems are instrumented well enough to support diagnosis, monitoring, and improvement.

controls.name: Rollback and Known Good Recovery
controls.description: Teams can return to a known good state quickly without inventing a recovery path during the incident.
evidence_examples: Rollback procedure used in the last 6 months
Known good artifact or environment reference
Rollback drill or real rollback evidence

scoring_criteria.red: Rollback or failover paths are missing or unreliable.
scoring_criteria.yellow: Rollback is possible in some cases but not consistently fast or safe.
scoring_criteria.green: Teams can reliably revert or fail over to a known good state when required.
